results.question: My landlord shut off my hot water to try to get me to move out. What can I do? (MA)
results.response:
If your landlord has shut off your hot water, this is considered illegal in Massachusetts. You have several options to address this situation:

1. **Go to Court for a Temporary Restraining Order (TRO):** You can immediately go to court to request a TRO. This order would require your landlord to restore your hot water and stop any illegal actions. You may also be entitled to money damages of at least three months' rent, plus any court costs and attorney's fees. [Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 12: Evictions (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/12%20Evictions%202025.pdf)]

2. **Call the Police:** Lockouts and utility shut-offs are crimes in Massachusetts. You can report the incident to the police. Sometimes, police intervention can resolve the issue quickly. [Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 12: Evictions (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/12%20Evictions%202025.pdf)]

3. **File a Lawsuit for Breach of Quiet Enjoyment:** You can sue your landlord for interfering with your utilities, which is a breach of your right to quiet enjoyment. If successful, you may be entitled to money damages equal to three months' rent or your actual losses, whichever is greater. [Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 13: When to Take Your Landlord to Court (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/13%20Taking%20Landlords%20to%20Court%202025.pdf)]

4. **Petition for Enforcement of the State Sanitary Code:** You can file a petition to ask the court to order your landlord to restore the hot water and make any necessary repairs. [Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 13: When to Take Your Landlord to Court (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/13%20Taking%20Landlords%20to%20Court%202025.pdf)]

For specific legal advice, it is recommended to consult with an attorney. You can also seek assistance from resources like MassLegalHelp.org or Greater Boston Legal Services.

---
**Sources:**
- [Legal Tactics Ch. 12: Evictions](https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/12%20Evictions%202025.pdf)
- [Legal Tactics Ch. 13: When to Take Your Landlord to Court](https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/13%20Taking%20Landlords%20to%20Court%202025.pdf)

results.question: I reported code violations and now my landlord is trying to evict me. Is this retaliation? (MA)
results.response:
In Massachusetts, it is illegal for a landlord to retaliate against a tenant for engaging in protected activities, such as reporting code violations to the landlord or the local board of health. If your landlord is attempting to evict you within six months of your reporting these violations, there is a legal presumption that the eviction is retaliatory. Retaliation can include actions like raising the rent, terminating or failing to renew a lease, or starting an eviction proceeding. 

If you believe you are facing retaliatory eviction, you may have a defense against the eviction. It is advisable to consult with an attorney to discuss your specific situation and explore your legal options. 

For more information, you can refer to the Attorney General's Guide to Landlord and Tenant Rights and Legal Tactics resources on MassLegalHelp.org. 

[Source: Attorney General's Guide to Landlord and Tenant Rights (https://www.mass.gov/guides/the-attorney-generals-guide-to-landlord-and-tenant-rights)]

results.question: What is the Section 8 housing program and how do I apply in Boston?
results.response:
The Section 8 housing program, also known as the Housing Choice Voucher Program, provides rent subsidies to assist eligible low-income families in obtaining decent, safe, and affordable housing. Families can choose housing within a neighborhood of their choice in privately owned housing and receive rental assistance. The Boston Housing Authority (BHA) administers this federally funded program. Rent subsidies, called vouchers, allow families to pay a reasonable share of their income toward rent, with the BHA covering the remaining portion. The program is named after Section 8 of the Housing and Community Development Act of 1974. [Source: Housing Options & Eligibility (https://www.bostonhousing.org/en/FAQ.aspx)]

As for applying in Boston, the Section 8 tenant-based voucher program waiting list is currently closed. However, new applicants may apply to other public housing programs, the Project-Based voucher program, and the Mod Rehab program. Applicants for the Project-Based Voucher and Mod Rehab programs must qualify for Priority One status. [Source: Housing Options & Eligibility (https://www.bostonhousing.org/en/FAQ.aspx)]

For more information or assistance, you might consider visiting resources like MassLegalHelp.org or contacting Greater Boston Legal Services.

results.question: My landlord never gave me a statement of condition for my security deposit. Does this matter? (MA)
results.response:
Yes, it does matter. In Massachusetts, if your landlord charged you a security deposit, they are required to provide you with a Statement of Condition within 10 days of your moving in. This statement should describe the condition of your rental unit and list any existing damage. If you do not receive this statement and there are serious problems in your rental unit, you should create your own Statement of Condition. This can help prevent your landlord from using your security deposit to pay for damages you did not cause. You can use a form like Statement of Condition (Form 3) to document this. 

If your landlord fails to provide the Statement of Condition, it can be a violation of the security deposit law, and you may have grounds to request the return of your security deposit. 

For more detailed guidance, you may want to consult legal aid resources such as MassLegalHelp.org or Greater Boston Legal Services.

[Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 3: Security Deposits (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-10/03%20Security%20Deposits%202025-%20Updated%208-13-25.pdf)]

results.question: Can my landlord charge me for bed bug treatment in Massachusetts?
results.response:
In Massachusetts, landlords are generally responsible for maintaining rental units in a habitable condition, which includes addressing pest infestations such as bed bugs. According to the City of Boston's guidelines, landlords must take responsibility for exterminating bed bugs and cannot charge tenants for the treatment if the infestation is not due to the tenant's actions. Tenants should notify their landlords immediately if they suspect a bed bug infestation. 

For more detailed information, you may want to consult resources like MassLegalHelp.org or Greater Boston Legal Services.

results.question: How do I request a reasonable accommodation for my disability from my landlord in Boston?
results.response:
To request a reasonable accommodation for your disability from your landlord in Boston, you should follow these steps:

1. **Identify Your Needs**: Clearly identify what accommodation you need due to your disability. This could be a change in policy, practice, or a physical modification to the property.

2. **Make a Written Request**: Submit your request in writing to your landlord. Clearly specify the accommodation you are seeking and explain how it relates to your disability.

3. **Engage in an Interactive Process**: Be prepared to engage in an interactive process with your landlord. This means working cooperatively to find a solution that meets your needs. Your landlord may request additional information to understand your request better.

4. **Provide Documentation if Necessary**: You may need to provide documentation from a healthcare provider that supports your need for the accommodation.

5. **Follow Up**: If you do not receive a response, follow up with your landlord to ensure your request is being considered.

If you need further assistance or face challenges in this process, you may consider reaching out to legal aid resources such as MassLegalHelp.org or Greater Boston Legal Services for guidance.

[Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 7: Discrimination (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/07%20Discrimination%202025.pdf)]

results.question: My apartment doesn't have working smoke detectors. Is my landlord required to install them? (MA)
results.response:
Yes, your landlord is required to install and maintain smoke detectors in your apartment. According to the Massachusetts State Sanitary Code, the owner must provide, install, and maintain operable smoke detectors and carbon monoxide alarms in every residence as required by law. This is in accordance with M.G.L. c. 148, §§ 26 C-F½ and other relevant codes. If there is a violation, such as non-working smoke detectors, it should be reported to the local board of health, which will notify the local fire department. 

[Source: 105 CMR 410 — Minimum Standards of Fitness for Human Habitation (State Sanitary Code Chapter II) (https://www.mass.gov/doc/105-cmr-410-minimum-standards-of-fitness-for-human-habitation-state-sanitary-code-chapter-ii/download)].

results.question: Can my landlord keep my security deposit for normal wear and tear in Massachusetts?
results.response:
No, in Massachusetts, a landlord cannot keep your security deposit for normal wear and tear. The security deposit can only be used to cover unpaid rent, unpaid water charges, tax escalation amounts, or to repair damages caused by the tenant or their guests that go beyond normal wear and tear. Routine painting, cleaning, or small repairs typically fall under normal wear and tear and cannot be deducted from the security deposit. 

If the landlord intends to deduct for damages, they must provide an itemized list of damages and repair costs, sworn under penalty of perjury, along with repair estimates or receipts, within 30 days after the tenancy ends. 

[Source: Massachusetts law about tenants' security deposits (https://www.mass.gov/info-details/massachusetts-law-about-tenants-security-deposits); Legal Tactics Ch. 3: Security Deposits (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-10/03%20Security%20Deposits%202025-%20Updated%208-13-25.pdf)]

- [Attorney General's Guide to Landlord and Tenant Rights](https://www.mass.gov/guides/the-attorney-generals-guide-to-landlord-and-tenant-rights)
- [Legal Tactics Ch. 3: Security Deposits](https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-10/03%20Security%20Deposits%202025-%20Updated%208-13-25.pdf)

results.question: What happens to my lease if my landlord sells the building in Massachusetts?
results.response:
If your landlord sells the building in Massachusetts, your lease generally remains in effect. The new owner takes over the responsibilities and obligations of the previous landlord under the existing lease agreement. This means that the terms of your lease, including the duration and rent amount, should not change simply because the property has been sold. 

For specific situations or concerns, it is advisable to consult an attorney. You can also find more information and resources at MassLegalHelp.org or Greater Boston Legal Services.

results.question: My landlord is deducting my security deposit for things that were broken when I moved in. What can I do? (MA)
results.response:
In Massachusetts, your landlord cannot deduct from your security deposit for damages that were already present when you moved in. Here are steps you can take:

1. **Documentation**: Ensure you have documentation, such as a Statement of Condition, photos, or any written communication, that proves the damages existed before you moved in.

2. **Demand Letter**: Send your landlord a demand letter requesting the return of your security deposit. Include evidence that the damages were pre-existing. Send this letter by certified mail, return receipt requested, and keep a copy for your records. [Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 3: Security Deposits (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-10/03%20Security%20Deposits%202025-%20Updated%208-13-25.pdf)]

3. **Legal Action**: If your landlord does not respond to your demand letter or refuses to return your deposit, you can take them to court. You may be entitled to up to three times the amount of the deposit, plus interest and court fees. [Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 3: Security Deposits (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-10/03%20Security%20Deposits%202025-%20Updated%208-13-25.pdf)]

results.question: Can I sue my landlord in small claims court for not returning my security deposit in MA?
results.response:
Yes, you can sue your landlord in small claims court in Massachusetts if they fail to return your security deposit. According to Massachusetts law, if your landlord does not return your security deposit within 30 days of the end of your tenancy, you may be entitled to sue for up to three times the amount of the deposit, plus interest and court fees. If you hire a lawyer, the landlord may also be required to pay your lawyer’s fees. [Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 3: Security Deposits (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-10/03%20Security%20Deposits%202025-%20Updated%208-13-25.pdf)].

For more information on filing a small claims case, you can visit the Massachusetts Court website: www.mass.gov/courts/selfhelp/small-claims/. If you need further assistance, consider reaching out to legal aid resources such as MassLegalHelp.org or Greater Boston Legal Services.

results.question: I just moved into a new apartment in Boston and my landlord didn't put my security deposit into a separate account. Is that allowed?
results.response:
No, that is not allowed. In Massachusetts, landlords are required to deposit security deposits in a separate bank account in a Massachusetts bank that collects interest. This account must be separate from the landlord's own funds and protected from the landlord's creditors. The landlord must also provide you with the name and address of the bank holding the security deposit and the account number. If the landlord fails to comply with these requirements, you may have the right to get your security deposit back.

results.question: My landlord stopped paying the electric bill, and I'm worried about losing service. What can I do according to Massachusetts law?
results.response:
In Massachusetts, if your landlord is responsible for paying the electric bill and fails to do so, resulting in a potential shut-off, this can be considered a violation of the state Sanitary Code and a breach of your right to quiet enjoyment. You have several options:

1. **Legal Action**: You may sue your landlord for money damages or court orders. If you can prove that the landlord's actions violated your quiet enjoyment, you may be entitled to money damages equal to either three months' rent or your actual loss, whichever is greater [Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 13: When to Take Your Landlord to Court (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/13%20Taking%20Landlords%20to%20Court%202025.pdf)].

2. **Receivership**: You can ask the court to appoint a temporary landlord, called a receiver, to manage the building and ensure utilities are paid [Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 6: Utilities (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-02/06%20Utilities%202025_0.pdf)].

3. **Protection Against Shut-offs**: If someone in your household is seriously ill, you may be protected against a utility shut-off. You should contact a medical professional to certify the illness to the utility company [Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 6: Utilities (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-02/06%20Utilities%202025_0.pdf)].

For specific guidance on your situation, it is recommended to consult with an attorney or seek assistance from legal aid resources such as MassLegalHelp.org or Greater Boston Legal Services.

---
**Sources:**
- [Legal Tactics Ch. 13: When to Take Your Landlord to Court](https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/13%20Taking%20Landlords%20to%20Court%202025.pdf)
- [Legal Tactics Ch. 6: Utilities](https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-02/06%20Utilities%202025_0.pdf)

results.question: I'm renting an apartment in Boston and my lease is for 12 months. What are my rights regarding the security deposit my landlord is holding?
results.response:
In Massachusetts, your rights regarding the security deposit are protected under the security deposit law. Here are some key points:

1. **Amount**: The security deposit cannot be more than your first month's rent. [Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 3: Security Deposits (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-10/03%20Security%20Deposits%202025-%20Updated%208-13-25.pdf)]

2. **Receipts and Records**: Your landlord must provide you with a written receipt within 30 days of receiving your security deposit. This receipt should indicate the bank where your security deposit is held. [Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 3: Security Deposits (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-10/03%20Security%20Deposits%202025-%20Updated%208-13-25.pdf)]

3. **Interest**: Your landlord is required to pay you interest on your security deposit annually. If they fail to do so, you can deduct the interest from your next month's rent. [Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 3: Security Deposits (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-10/03%20Security%20Deposits%202025-%20Updated%208-13-25.pdf)]

4. **Return of Deposit**: At the end of your tenancy, if there is no damage to the apartment and no unpaid rent, your landlord must return your security deposit plus any interest owed within 30 days. If there is damage, the landlord must provide a detailed list of damages within 30 days. [Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 3: Security Deposits (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-10/03%20Security%20Deposits%202025-%20Updated%208-13-25.pdf)]

5. **Violations**: If your landlord fails to comply with the security deposit law, you may be entitled to the return of your deposit and possibly three times the amount of the deposit if certain violations occur, such as not placing the deposit in a separate account. [Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 3: Security Deposits (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-10/03%20Security%20Deposits%202025-%20Updated%208-13-25.pdf)]

If you have specific concerns or issues with your landlord regarding your security deposit, it is advisable to consult with an attorney or seek assistance from legal aid resources such as MassLegalHelp.org or Greater Boston Legal Services.

---

- [Legal Tactics Ch. 3: Security Deposits](https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-10/03%20Security%20Deposits%202025-%20Updated%208-13-25.pdf)

results.question: I just found out my landlord is selling the building. What happens to my security deposit?
results.response:
If your landlord is selling the building, your security deposit should be transferred to the new owner. The new landlord has 45 days to send you a notice about your security deposit, last month's rent, and any interest owed. If the new landlord does not send you this notice by the deadline, the old landlord must return your deposit plus interest. 

If the new landlord asks you to pay another security deposit or last month’s rent, you should refuse and inform them that you have already paid the previous landlord. Under Massachusetts law, a tenant cannot be required to pay a new or additional security deposit or last month's rent payment because someone else has taken over the building. 

In most cases, the new owner is responsible for your security deposit, last month’s rent, and interest, even if they never received it from the old landlord. If the old landlord does not return your money, you can sue them for three times the amount they owe you. 

For more detailed guidance, you may want to consult resources such as MassLegalHelp.org or Greater Boston Legal Services. 

[Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 3: Security Deposits (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-10/03%20Security%20Deposits%202025-%20Updated%208-13-25.pdf)]

---

results.question: Can my landlord in Boston evict me because I reported a building code violation to the city?
results.response:
No, your landlord cannot legally evict you for reporting a building code violation to the city. Under Massachusetts law, it is illegal for a landlord to retaliate against a tenant for reporting violations of health or building codes. This protection is outlined in MGL c.186, s.18, which states that any reprisal against a tenant for such actions is prohibited and can result in the landlord being liable for damages. If you receive a notice of termination of tenancy or any substantial alteration in the terms of your tenancy within six months after reporting a violation, it creates a rebuttable presumption that the action is retaliatory.

For more information, you can refer to the source: [Source: MGL c.186 s.18 - Anti-Retaliation Protection for Tenants (https://malegislature.gov/Laws/GeneralLaws/PartII/TitleI/Chapter186/Section18)]. If you need further assistance, consider consulting legal aid resources such as MassLegalHelp.org or Greater Boston Legal Services.

results.question: What steps should I take to prepare for an eviction court hearing in Boston?
results.response:
To prepare for an eviction court hearing in Boston, you should follow these steps:

1. **Understand the Eviction Process**: Familiarize yourself with the eviction process in Massachusetts. A landlord cannot evict you without going to court and obtaining permission from a judge. [Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 12: Evictions (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/12%20Evictions%202025.pdf)]

2. **Prepare Your Case**: 
   - Collect necessary documents such as rent receipts, pictures of any bad conditions, and any other evidence that supports your case.
   - Prepare a list of questions you want to ask your landlord.
   - Notify any witnesses of the time and place they should be in court.
   - Prepare a brief statement summarizing how the landlord violated the law and why you should not be evicted. [Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 12: Evictions (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/12%20Evictions%202025.pdf)]

3. **Consider Transferring Your Case**: You might want to transfer your case to the Boston Housing Court, which specializes in eviction cases. To do this, complete a Notice to Transfer form and file it with the District Court, the Housing Court, and your landlord (or their attorney) by the day before the original court date. [Source: Boston Housing Court (https://www.boston.gov/boston-housing-court)]

4. **Prepare for Court**:
   - Plan to spend the day in court as it is difficult to know when your case will be called.
   - Answer when your name is called to avoid being defaulted.
   - Be patient, as you may have to wait for hours before your case is called. [Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 12: Evictions (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/12%20Evictions%202025.pdf)]

5. **Seek Legal Help**: Consider getting legal help from resources such as Greater Boston Legal Services, Harvard Legal Aid Bureau, or the Volunteer Lawyer’s Project. [Source: Boston Housing Court (https://www.boston.gov/boston-housing-court)]

For more detailed information, you can visit MassLegalHelp.org or consult with an attorney for specific legal advice.

---
**Sources:**
- [Legal Tactics Ch. 12: Evictions](https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/12%20Evictions%202025.pdf)
- [What Happens During an Eviction](https://www.boston.gov/departments/housing/what-happens-during-eviction)
- [Boston Housing Court](https://www.boston.gov/boston-housing-court)

results.question: Can I withhold rent if my apartment in Boston has serious code violations?
results.response:
Yes, you can withhold rent if your apartment in Boston has serious code violations, but there are specific steps you must follow to do so legally. According to Massachusetts law, tenants have the right to withhold rent if there are conditions in the apartment that violate the state Sanitary Code and affect the health or safety of the occupants. Here are the steps you should take:

1. **Document the Conditions**: Make sure to document the bad conditions. A housing inspection report that describes code violations is the best way to document these issues. Taking pictures or videos with a date and time stamp can also be helpful.

2. **Notify Your Landlord**: Use a written notice, such as the sample Rent Withholding Letter (Form 12), to inform your landlord about the bad conditions.

3. **Deposit Rent in a Bank**: While withholding rent, deposit the rent money in a separate bank account to show that you are not simply avoiding payment.

4. **Meet Legal Requirements**: Ensure you meet all the legal requirements for withholding rent. This includes notifying the landlord and documenting the conditions.

If your landlord decides to take you to court to recover the withheld rent, you will need to explain to the judge how the conditions affected you. If you have followed all the legal requirements, you may have a defense against eviction and could potentially keep part or all of the withheld rent. However, the court may order you to pay ongoing rent into an escrow account while the case is pending.

For more detailed guidance, it is advisable to consult an attorney or refer to resources like MassLegalHelp.org or Greater Boston Legal Services.

[Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 8: Getting Repairs Made (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/08%20Getting%20Repairs%20Made%202025_0.pdf)]

results.question: Can I withhold rent in Boston if my landlord hasn't fixed the heating in my apartment?
results.response:
Yes, you may be able to withhold rent in Boston if your landlord hasn't fixed the heating in your apartment, provided certain conditions are met. Under Massachusetts law, tenants have the right to withhold rent if the landlord fails to provide safe and habitable housing, which includes adequate heating.

Before you can legally withhold rent, you must ensure the following conditions are met:

1. Defective conditions exist in your apartment.
2. These conditions "endanger or materially impair" the health, safety, or well-being of anyone living in the home.
3. The landlord knows about the defective conditions and knew this before you were behind in your rent.
4. The conditions were not caused by you, a household member, guest, or other person under your control.
5. The landlord can make repairs without you having to permanently move out.

If you answer yes to all these questions, you can legally withhold your rent. It is important to document the conditions and communicate with your landlord about the issues. Additionally, it is recommended to set aside the withheld rent in a separate bank account to demonstrate that you have the funds available if required by a court later.

For more detailed guidance, you can refer to the resources provided by MassLegalHelp.org or consult with an attorney. [Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 8: Getting Repairs Made (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/08%20Getting%20Repairs%20Made%202025_0.pdf)]

results.question: I just got an eviction notice in Boston. When do I need to appear in court, and what happens if I don't respond in time?
results.response:
When you receive an eviction notice, the eviction process begins with a Notice to Quit. If the landlord proceeds with the eviction, they will serve you with a Summons and Complaint, which will include the date and time you need to appear in court. This is known as the First Tier Court Event in housing court or the Case Management Conference in district court. It is crucial to appear on this date, as it is a mandatory mediation session where you and your landlord will discuss the possibility of resolving the case by agreement. If you do not appear, you will be defaulted, which means you automatically lose your case, and the eviction will proceed in favor of the landlord. [Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 12: Evictions (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/12%20Evictions%202025.pdf)].

If you fail to appear in court on the scheduled date, the judge will rule in favor of the landlord, and the eviction will proceed. It is important to attend court to have your side of the story heard. [Source: What Happens During an Eviction (https://www.boston.gov/departments/housing/what-happens-during-eviction)].

For more detailed guidance or if you need legal assistance, consider reaching out to resources like MassLegalHelp.org or Greater Boston Legal Services.

results.question: My apartment in Boston has been unbearably hot this winter, constantly over 78°F. Is my landlord violating any regulations?
results.response:
Massachusetts law requires landlords to provide a habitable living environment, which includes maintaining appropriate heating conditions. While there are specific regulations about minimum temperatures that must be maintained during the heating season, there are no specific maximum temperature regulations. However, if the excessive heat is causing the apartment to be uninhabitable, it may be considered a violation of the warranty of habitability.

You may want to discuss the issue with your landlord to see if a solution can be reached. If the problem persists, you could consider contacting the local health department for an inspection to determine if the conditions violate the State Sanitary Code.

results.question: Can my landlord in Boston be penalized if they don't fix a serious health code violation in my apartment after being ordered to?
results.response:
Yes, your landlord in Boston can be penalized if they do not fix a serious health code violation after being ordered to do so. If a landlord fails to comply with the state Sanitary Code, fines can range from $10 to $500 per day for violations. Additionally, if the landlord fails to provide essential services like heat or hot water, or interferes with your quiet enjoyment, they may face criminal penalties, including fines and potentially up to 6 months in jail for violating the law of quiet enjoyment. 

If the landlord does not follow a court order to make repairs, they may be held in contempt of court, which can result in further penalties.

results.question: Can my landlord block the hallway with furniture in our apartment building in Boston?
results.response:
No, your landlord cannot block the hallway with furniture in your apartment building. Blocking hallways can violate the Massachusetts State Sanitary Code, which requires that all means of egress (exits) be kept clear and unobstructed to ensure safe passage in case of emergencies. This is part of the landlord's obligation to maintain a safe and well-maintained property. If you encounter such a situation, you may want to contact your local health department or a housing inspector to report the issue.

For more information, you can refer to the Massachusetts State Sanitary Code or consult resources like MassLegalHelp.org for guidance on tenant rights and landlord obligations. results.question: My landlord in Boston is trying to keep my entire security deposit after I accidentally damaged a wall. Is this allowed?
results.response:
In Massachusetts, a landlord can only use a security deposit to cover certain costs, such as unpaid rent or damages caused by the tenant beyond reasonable wear and tear. If you accidentally damaged a wall, the landlord may be able to deduct the cost of repairing that damage from your security deposit. However, they cannot keep the entire deposit unless the repair costs equal or exceed the deposit amount.

The landlord must provide you with an itemized list of damages and repair costs, sworn under penalty of perjury, along with receipts or estimates for the repairs, within 30 days after the end of your tenancy. If they fail to do so, you may be entitled to the return of your deposit.

If you believe the landlord is wrongfully withholding your deposit, you can send a Security Deposit Demand Letter and, if necessary, file a claim in small claims court. You may be entitled to triple damages if the landlord fails to comply with certain legal requirements regarding the handling of your security deposit.

For more detailed guidance, you may want to consult resources like MassLegalHelp.org or Greater Boston Legal Services.

[Source: Massachusetts law about tenants' security deposits (https://www.mass.gov/info-details/massachusetts-law-about-tenants-security-deposits)]

results.question: Can my landlord shut off my electricity if I’m behind on rent payments in Boston?
results.response:
No, your landlord cannot shut off your electricity if you are behind on rent payments. In Massachusetts, it is illegal for a landlord to force a tenant out by shutting off utilities. This is considered a breach of the tenant's right to quiet enjoyment and is a criminal offense. results.question: Can my landlord in Boston refuse to rent to me because I have children?
results.response:
No, your landlord in Boston cannot refuse to rent to you because you have children. Families with children are protected from discrimination under fair housing laws. This protection also extends to people who are pregnant, in the process of adopting a child, getting custody or guardianship of a child, or providing foster care. It is illegal for landlords to refuse to rent to you or discriminate against you because you have or are expecting children. 

However, there are some exceptions where Massachusetts landlords may exclude families with children, such as in two-family homes where the owners are occupants and certain buildings intended for people over 55 years old. Even in these cases, landlords cannot advertise or make statements that they will not rent to children. 

Additionally, landlords cannot refuse to rent to you because an apartment has lead paint. They are required to delead the apartment if you have a child under the age of six. 

[Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 7: Discrimination (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/07%20Discrimination%202025.pdf)]

results.question: I heard Boston has some new rules about affordable housing requirements for developers. Can someone explain what these are?
results.response:
Boston has specific policies regarding the treatment of affordable units in mixed-income developments. The Mayor’s Office of Housing (MOH) has established guidelines to ensure that income-restricted units are comparable to market-rate units in terms of access, size, design, finishes, and quality. Here are some key points:

1. **Distribution and Comparability**: Income-restricted units must be evenly distributed throughout the building and cannot be clustered or segregated. They should be indistinguishable from market-rate units in terms of exterior and interior finishes, and all residents must have equal access to building amenities and common areas.

2. **Unit Size and Layout**: Income-restricted units must be distributed by bedroom size in the same proportion as the unit mix in the development. The size and layout of these units should be comparable to market-rate units.

3. **Construction Schedule**: Income-restricted units should be constructed and occupied earlier than or at least concurrently with market-rate units, although exceptions may be made under exceptional circumstances.

These policies are part of the Inclusionary Development Policy and aim to ensure equitable housing opportunities for all residents. For more detailed information, you can refer to the Mayor’s Office of Housing guidelines. [Source: Sites Default Files File 2023 01 Comparable 20Housing 20Policy 10.1.19 (https://www.boston.gov/sites/default/files/file/2023/01/Comparable%20Housing%20Policy_10.1.19.pdf)].

results.question: I'm a renter in Boston and my landlord wants to switch my lease to a month-to-month agreement. What are the pros and cons of this change?
results.response:
Switching from a lease to a month-to-month tenancy, also known as a tenancy-at-will, has both advantages and disadvantages:

**Pros:**
1. **Flexibility:** A month-to-month agreement offers more flexibility. You can move out with less notice, typically one rental period (usually 30 days), without being tied to a long-term commitment. [Source: Top Ten Things Tenants and Landlords Need to Know (https://www.boston.gov/departments/housing/top-ten-things-tenants-and-landlords-need-know)]

2. **Adaptability:** If your circumstances change, such as needing to relocate for a job, you can do so more easily without breaking a lease.

**Cons:**
1. **Less Security:** A lease provides more security as it locks in your rent and terms for a set period, typically one year. With a month-to-month agreement, your landlord can change the terms, including rent, with proper notice (usually 30 days). [Source: Top Ten Things Tenants and Landlords Need to Know (https://www.boston.gov/departments/housing/top-ten-things-tenants-and-landlords-need-know)]

2. **Potential for Frequent Changes:** The landlord can terminate the tenancy with proper notice, which could lead to more frequent moves if the landlord decides to end the tenancy or increase the rent.

For specific advice regarding your situation, it is recommended to consult with an attorney or seek assistance from resources like MassLegalHelp.org or Greater Boston Legal Services.

---
**Sources:**
- [Top Ten Things Tenants and Landlords Need to Know](https://www.boston.gov/departments/housing/top-ten-things-tenants-and-landlords-need-know)
- [Legal Tactics Ch. 5: Rent](https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-02/05%20Rent%202025%20v2.pdf)
- [Legal Tactics Ch. 11: Moving Out](https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/11%20Moving%20Out%202025.pdf)

results.question: Can my landlord make me pay a broker fee if I'm renting an apartment in Boston?
results.response:
No, your landlord cannot make you pay a broker fee if you are renting an apartment in Boston, unless you hired the broker yourself. According to a law effective August 1, 2025, property owners in Massachusetts cannot require renters to pay broker’s fees when the broker was hired by the landlord. If the landlord hired the broker, they must pay the fee. You are only responsible for the broker fee if you hired the broker and agreed to it in writing. [Source: Broker Fees: What To Know About The New Law (https://www.boston.gov/departments/housing/office-housing-stability/broker-fees-3-things-know-about-new-law)].

results.question: I heard my apartment building is being foreclosed. I have a lease with a rental subsidy. What happens to my lease after foreclosure?
results.response:
If your apartment building is being foreclosed and you have a lease with a rental subsidy, your tenancy is protected. According to Massachusetts law, foreclosure does not affect the tenancy agreement of a tenant whose rental payment is subsidized under state or federal law. The foreclosing entity must assume the lease and rental subsidy contract with the rental subsidy administrator. This means that your lease should continue under the same terms after the foreclosure. 

[Source: MGL c.186 s.13A - Tenants at will upon foreclosure (https://malegislature.gov/Laws/GeneralLaws/PartII/TitleI/Chapter186/Section13A)].

Additionally, under MGL c.186A, a foreclosing owner cannot evict a tenant except for just cause or unless a binding purchase and sale agreement has been executed for a bona fide third party to purchase the housing accommodation from a foreclosing owner. 

[Source: MGL c.186A - Tenant Protections in Foreclosed Properties (https://malegislature.gov/Laws/GeneralLaws/PartII/TitleI/Chapter186A)].

For more detailed information or specific legal advice, it is recommended to consult an attorney or seek assistance from legal aid resources such as MassLegalHelp.org or Greater Boston Legal Services.

---

- [Legal Tactics Ch. 3: Security Deposits](https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-10/03%20Security%20Deposits%202025-%20Updated%208-13-25.pdf)

results.question: I'm renting an apartment in Boston with lead paint, and I'm concerned about safety for my child. How can I get help to address this issue?
results.response:
If you are renting an apartment in Boston and are concerned about lead paint, especially if you have a child under the age of 6, there are several steps you can take to address this issue:

1. **Request a Lead Inspection**: You can ask your landlord to have the apartment tested for lead. If they do not schedule a licensed lead inspector, you can request a free lead inspection from your local Board of Health or the Childhood Lead Poisoning Prevention Program (CLPPP). You can also perform a home lead test yourself or hire a licensed lead inspector. If lead is found, the landlord must reimburse you for the inspection cost. [Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 9: Lead Poisoning]

2. **Notify Your Landlord**: If lead paint is found, immediately inform your landlord in writing that you want the lead hazards removed. This written notice is important to protect yourself in case the landlord tries to retaliate against you. [Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 9: Lead Poisoning]

3. **Legal Protections**: Massachusetts law prohibits landlords from evicting you, increasing your rent, or refusing to renew your lease in retaliation for reporting lead paint violations. If a court finds that your landlord has retaliated, they may be ordered to pay you up to three months' rent. [Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 9: Lead Poisoning]

4. **Deleading Process**: If lead hazards are identified, your landlord is required to remove or cover them. This process is known as deleading, and it must be done by licensed professionals unless the landlord has special permission to do it themselves. [Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 9: Lead Poisoning]

5. **Temporary Housing**: If you need to move out during the deleading process, your landlord must pay for reasonable moving expenses and temporary housing costs. If the temporary housing is more expensive than your regular rent, the landlord is responsible for the difference. [Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 9: Lead Poisoning]

6. **Legal Action**: If your child has been poisoned by lead in your apartment, you have the right to sue the landlord for the injuries your child has suffered. You can file such a case until your child turns 21. It is advisable to consult with a lawyer experienced in lead poisoning cases. [Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 9: Lead Poisoning]

For further assistance, you may want to contact legal aid resources such as MassLegalHelp.org or Greater Boston Legal Services.

---
**Sources:**
- [Legal Tactics Ch. 9: Lead Poisoning](https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/09%20Lead%20Poisoning%202025.pdf)

results.question: I'm renting an apartment in Boston and concerned about lead paint. How can I get help to remove it?
results.response:
If you are concerned about lead paint in your apartment, here are the steps you can take:

1. **Notify Your Landlord**: If you notice peeling or chipping paint, inform your landlord in writing immediately. Request that they have the apartment tested for lead.

2. **Lead Inspection**: If your landlord does not arrange for a lead inspection, you can:
   - Request a free lead inspection from your local Board of Health or the Childhood Lead Poisoning Prevention Program.

results.question: I'm renting an apartment in Boston with peeling paint, and I suspect it has lead. How can I address this issue?
results.response:
If you suspect that your apartment has lead due to peeling paint, you should take the following steps:

1. **Notify Your Landlord**: Immediately inform your landlord in writing about the peeling paint and your concerns about potential lead hazards. Keep a dated copy of this notification for your records.

2. **Request a Lead Inspection**: If you have a child under 6 years old living with you, you are entitled to request a lead inspection from your local Board of Health or the Childhood Lead Poisoning Prevention Program. They are required to conduct the inspection within 10 days of your request. If you encounter any issues in getting an inspection, contact the Childhood Lead Poisoning Prevention Program (CLPPP) for assistance.

3. **Conduct a Home Test**: You can also purchase a home lead test kit from a hardware store or online to test for lead yourself. If lead is found, your landlord is responsible for reimbursing you for the cost of the inspection.

4. **Landlord's Responsibility**: If lead is detected, your landlord must remove or cover any dangerous levels of lead. This process is known as deleading, and it usually requires a licensed professional. Your landlord may need special permission to do the work themselves.

5. **Temporary Housing During Deleading**: If you need to move out during the deleading process, your landlord is responsible for paying reasonable moving expenses and any additional costs if the temporary housing is more expensive than your regular rent.

6. **Protection Against Retaliation**: Massachusetts law prohibits landlords from retaliating against tenants for reporting lead hazards. If your landlord attempts to evict you or increase your rent in retaliation, you may have legal recourse.

For more detailed information, you can refer to the Massachusetts Lead Law and resources like MassLegalHelp.org or contact Greater Boston Legal Services for assistance.

results.question: I'm renting an apartment in Boston and my landlord says I have to pay for my own heat. Is that legal?
results.response:
In Boston, a tenant can be required to pay for their own heat if there is a separate meter for the heating service and there is a written agreement stating that the tenant is responsible for paying for heat. If these conditions are not met, the landlord is typically responsible for providing heat. Additionally, landlords who pay for heat must provide it between September 15 and May 31. 

For more detailed information or if you have specific concerns, you may want to consult an attorney or refer to resources like MassLegalHelp.org or Greater Boston Legal Services.

[Source: Top Ten Things Tenants and Landlords Need to Know (https://www.boston.gov/departments/housing/top-ten-things-tenants-and-landlords-need-know)]

results.question: Can I ask for a modification to my lease terms due to my disability if I'm struggling to meet certain requirements?
results.response:
Yes, you can request a modification to your lease terms as a reasonable accommodation if you are struggling to meet certain requirements due to your disability. According to Massachusetts tenant law, a tenant can request an accommodation or modification from their landlord or property manager to address disability-related needs. This request can be made verbally or in writing, although it is best practice to make the request in writing to have a record. The request should clearly indicate that the change is needed because of a disability. 

The landlord is required to consider the request and may verify the disability and the need for the accommodation. If the request is reasonable and does not pose an undue financial or administrative burden, the landlord should approve it. If there are concerns about the burden or a fundamental alteration, the landlord should engage in an interactive process with the tenant to find a suitable alternative accommodation. 

For more information, you can refer to the following source: [Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 7: Discrimination (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/07%20Discrimination%202025.pdf)].

If you need further assistance, consider reaching out to legal aid resources such as MassLegalHelp.org or Greater Boston Legal Services.

results.question: I requested a specific accommodation for my disability from the Boston Housing Authority, but they offered me a different one. Can they do that?
results.response:
Yes, the Boston Housing Authority (BHA) can offer a different accommodation than the one you requested. The process of determining reasonable accommodations involves an interactive dialogue between you and the housing provider. If the BHA believes that the requested accommodation poses an undue administrative and financial burden, results in a fundamental alteration, or if there is a more suitable alternative, they may propose a different accommodation that still meets your needs. This is part of the interactive process to find a resolution that accommodates your disability without imposing undue burdens on the housing provider. 

For more detailed guidance, you may want to consult legal aid resources such as MassLegalHelp.org or Greater Boston Legal Services. 

[Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 7: Discrimination (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/07%20Discrimination%202025.pdf)]

results.question: Is my landlord required to provide a refrigerator in my Boston apartment?
results.response:
In Boston, landlords are not universally required to provide a refrigerator in an apartment. However, if a refrigerator is provided, the landlord is responsible for maintaining it. results.question: Can my landlord enter my apartment without my permission in Boston?
results.response:
In Boston, your landlord generally cannot enter your apartment without your permission. They must provide advance notice and arrange a convenient time to enter for purposes such as inspections, repairs, or showing the apartment to prospective tenants. The standard notice period is at least 24 hours. However, there are exceptions where a landlord may enter without permission, such as in the case of a mechanical or repair emergency that could damage the entire building, or if it appears the tenant has abandoned the apartment. results.question: My landlord hasn't returned my security deposit and it's been over 30 days since I moved out. What are my rights in Massachusetts?
results.response:
In Massachusetts, if your landlord has not returned your security deposit within 30 days after you move out, you have certain rights. According to Massachusetts law, the landlord must return your security deposit, along with any interest owed, within 30 days of the end of your tenancy. If they fail to do so, you can take the following steps:

1. **Demand Letter**: You can send a demand letter to your landlord requesting the return of your security deposit. This letter should be sent by certified mail, return receipt requested, and you should keep a copy for your records. The demand letter gives your landlord a chance to return your deposit without having to go to court. [Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 3: Security Deposits (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-10/03%20Security%20Deposits%202025-%20Updated%208-13-25.pdf)]

2. **Legal Action**: If your landlord does not respond to your demand letter, you can take them to small claims court. You can ask the court for up to three times the amount of the deposit, plus interest, and any court fees. If you hire a lawyer, the landlord may also be required to pay your lawyer’s fees. [Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 3: Security Deposits (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-10/03%20Security%20Deposits%202025-%20Updated%208-13-25.pdf)]

For more detailed guidance, you may want to consult an attorney or seek assistance from legal aid resources such as MassLegalHelp.org or Greater Boston Legal Services.

---
**Sources:**
- [Legal Tactics Ch. 3: Security Deposits](https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-10/03%20Security%20Deposits%202025-%20Updated%208-13-25.pdf)

results.question: Can my landlord charge me first, last, security deposit AND a broker fee when I move in? (MA)

results.question: My apartment has mold and my landlord won't fix it. What can I do? (Boston)
results.response:
If your apartment has mold and your landlord refuses to fix it, you have several options under Massachusetts tenant law:

1. **Notify Your Landlord**: Inform your landlord about the mold issue in writing (email or letter) to have a record of your communication. This is important for documenting the problem and your efforts to have it resolved.

2. **Contact the Board of Health**: If your landlord does not address the issue, you can contact your local Board of Health to request an inspection. A housing inspector can assess whether the mold violates the Massachusetts Sanitary Code and can order your landlord to make necessary repairs within a specific timeframe.

3. **Document the Problem**: Take photos or videos of the mold to have evidence of the condition. This documentation can be useful if you need to take further action.

4. **Withhold Rent**: You may have the right to withhold rent until the landlord makes the necessary repairs. However, you should place the withheld rent in a separate bank account and notify your landlord in writing about your intention to withhold rent due to the mold issue. It's important to follow legal procedures to protect yourself from eviction.

5. **Repair and Deduct**: You might be able to make the repairs yourself and deduct the cost from your rent. This option requires following specific legal steps, including obtaining a Board of Health report.

6. **Legal Action**: If the issue persists, you may consider taking your landlord to court. A judge could order the landlord to make repairs or compensate you for the bad conditions.

For more detailed guidance, you can refer to resources like MassLegalHelp.org or consider consulting with an attorney to understand your rights and options fully.

[Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 8: Getting Repairs Made (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/08%20Getting%20Repairs%20Made%202025_0.pdf)]

results.question: Can my landlord evict me in winter in Massachusetts?
results.response:
In Massachusetts, a landlord can initiate eviction proceedings at any time of the year, including winter. However, the eviction process must follow legal procedures, and tenants have rights that protect them during this process. For example, a landlord cannot evict a tenant without a court order, and the tenant must be given proper notice and an opportunity to contest the eviction in court.

If you are facing eviction, it is important to understand your rights and the legal process. You may want to consult with an attorney or seek assistance from legal aid organizations such as MassLegalHelp.org or Greater Boston Legal Services for guidance specific to your situation.

results.question: My landlord is raising my rent by $500/month with only 2 weeks notice. Is this legal in MA?
results.response:
In Massachusetts, the legality of a rent increase depends on whether you have a lease or are a tenant at will.

1. **Tenants with a Lease**: If you have a lease, your landlord cannot raise your rent before the lease period ends without your agreement. If they want to raise your rent for the next lease period, they must give you a notice that ends your tenancy before the lease renews or extends. This is typically done through a "Notice to Quit." [Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 5: Rent (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-02/05%20Rent%202025%20v2.pdf)]

2. **Tenants at Will**: If you do not have a lease (tenant at will), your landlord must provide at least 30 days' advance written notice to increase your rent. The exact notice required depends on how frequently and on what day you pay rent. [Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 5: Rent (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-02/05%20Rent%202025%20v2.pdf)]

Therefore, a 2-week notice for a rent increase is not sufficient for either situation. You may want to consult with a legal aid organization such as MassLegalHelp.org or Greater Boston Legal Services for further assistance.

results.question: Can I withhold rent if my landlord won't make repairs in Massachusetts?
results.response:
Yes, in Massachusetts, tenants have the right to withhold rent if their landlord fails to make necessary repairs, provided certain conditions are met. This is based on the landlord's obligation to provide safe and habitable housing under the warranty of habitability. Here are the key points to consider:

1. **Conditions for Withholding Rent**: Before you can legally withhold rent, you must ensure:
   - Defective conditions exist in your apartment.
   - These conditions endanger or materially impair the health, safety, or well-being of anyone living in the home.
   - The landlord knows about the defective conditions before you were behind in your rent.
   - The conditions were not caused by you, a household member, guest, or someone under your control.
   - Repairs can be made without you having to permanently move out.

2. **Documentation**: It is crucial to document the bad conditions and communicate with your landlord in writing. This can include taking photos or videos and keeping records of all communications.

3. **Setting Aside Rent**: While withholding rent, it is advisable to set aside the withheld rent in a separate bank account. This demonstrates to the court that you have the money to pay rent and strengthens your case if the landlord attempts to evict you.

4. **Legal Process**: If the landlord takes you to court, you can explain how the conditions affected you and your family. A judge may decide you can keep part or all of the withheld rent depending on the situation.

5. **Risks**: Be prepared for the possibility that your landlord may try to evict you. However, if you have properly withheld your rent, your landlord may be violating other laws, such as retaliatory eviction laws.

For more detailed guidance, you can refer to resources like MassLegalHelp.org or consult with a tenant advocacy organization or lawyer. 

[Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 8: Getting Repairs Made (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/08%20Getting%20Repairs%20Made%202025_0.pdf)]

results.question: My landlord didn't put my security deposit in a separate bank account. Can I get triple damages?
results.response:
Yes, under Massachusetts law, if your landlord fails to deposit your security deposit in a separate, interest-bearing account in a Massachusetts bank, you may be entitled to triple damages. According to G. L. c. 186, s.15B, a tenant is entitled to three times the amount of the security deposit, plus interest, court costs, and attorney's fees if the landlord does not comply with the security deposit requirements, such as failing to deposit the security deposit in the proper bank account.

results.question: I want to break my lease early in Massachusetts. What are the consequences?
results.response:
Breaking a lease early in Massachusetts can have several consequences, but there are also options to mitigate potential liabilities. Here are some key points to consider:

1. **Lease Obligations**: By signing a lease, you agree to pay rent for the entire lease term. If you leave before the lease ends, you may be responsible for the rent for the remainder of the lease period. [Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 11: Moving Out (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/11%20Moving%20Out%202025.pdf)]

2. **Landlord Agreement**: You can negotiate with your landlord to agree on an earlier move-out date. If the landlord agrees, ensure you get a written acceptance of the "surrender" of the apartment. This can release you from further rent obligations. [Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 11: Moving Out (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/11%20Moving%20Out%202025.pdf)]

3. **Bad Conditions**: If there are serious sanitary code violations or unsafe conditions that the landlord fails to repair, you may have the right to break the lease. You must follow specific legal steps, including having a housing inspector document the conditions. [Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 8: Getting Repairs Made (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/08%20Getting%20Repairs%20Made%202025_0.pdf)]

4. **Subletting**: If your lease allows, you may sublet the apartment. However, you remain responsible for the lease, meaning if the subletter fails to pay rent or damages the property, you could be held liable. [Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 11: Moving Out (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/11%20Moving%20Out%202025.pdf)]

5. **Special Circumstances**: If you are a survivor of domestic violence, stalking, or rape, you may have additional rights to break your lease. [Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 11: Moving Out (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/11%20Moving%20Out%202025.pdf)]

For specific legal advice tailored to your situation, it is recommended to consult with an attorney. You can also find more resources at MassLegalHelp.org or contact Greater Boston Legal Services.

results.question: Can my landlord refuse to rent to me because I have kids? (Massachusetts)
results.response:
No, a landlord in Massachusetts cannot refuse to rent to you because you have children. This is considered discrimination based on family status, which is prohibited under both Massachusetts law and the Federal Fair Housing Act. Specifically, landlords cannot refuse to rent to you or evict you if you have children, even if the property has lead paint. They are responsible for correcting any lead paint hazards in the home. 

If you believe you have been discriminated against, you can contact the Boston Office of Fair Housing & Equity or the Massachusetts Commission Against Discrimination for assistance. 

[Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 9: Lead Poisoning (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/09%20Lead%20Poisoning%202025.pdf); Legal Tactics Ch. 7: Discrimination (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/07%20Discrimination%202025.pdf)]

results.question: My landlord changed the locks while I was away for a week. Is this an illegal eviction in MA?
results.response:
Yes, changing the locks without a court order is considered an illegal eviction in Massachusetts. This is known as a "lockout," and it is illegal for a landlord to evict a tenant through self-help tactics such as changing the locks without a court's permission. If your landlord has done this, they may be violating both civil and criminal laws.

You can take the following steps:

1. **Write a Demand Letter**: Inform your landlord that they are committing an illegal act and that you will take legal action to enforce your rights if they do not rectify the situation.

2. **Go to Court**: You can seek a temporary restraining order (TRO) from the court. A TRO can order your landlord to stop the illegal action and restore you to your apartment, including changing the locks back and restoring any utilities if they were shut off.

You may also be entitled to money damages of at least three months' rent, plus any court costs and attorney's fees.

For more detailed guidance, you may want to consult with an attorney or contact legal aid resources such as MassLegalHelp.org or Greater Boston Legal Services.

[Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 12: Evictions (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/12%20Evictions%202025.pdf)]

results.question: What is the eviction process in Massachusetts? How long does it take?
results.response:
In Massachusetts, the eviction process involves several steps that a landlord must follow to legally evict a tenant. Here's a general overview of the process:

1. **Notice to Quit**: The landlord must provide the tenant with a written notice, called a Notice to Quit, which specifies when and why they want the tenant to move out. The notice period depends on the reason for eviction:
   - 7-Day Notice: For certain lease violations.
   - 14-Day Notice: For non-payment of rent.
   - 30-Day Notice: For no specific reason or other violations.
   - No Notice: If the landlord accuses the tenant of illegal activity.
   [Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 12: Evictions (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/12%20Evictions%202025.pdf)]

2. **Summons and Complaint**: If the tenant does not move out by the date specified in the Notice to Quit, the landlord must have a constable or sheriff serve the tenant with a Summons and Complaint. This starts the legal process to obtain permission from the court to evict the tenant.
   [Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 12: Evictions (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/12%20Evictions%202025.pdf)]

3. **Court Process**: The court will schedule a date for a First Tier Court Event, which is a mandatory mediation session. If the case is not resolved, it will proceed to trial. The court must notify the tenant of the trial date, and the tenant has the right to defend themselves, file counterclaims, and request a jury trial.
   [Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 12: Evictions (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/12%20Evictions%202025.pdf)]

4. **Eviction Timeline**: The entire process can take several weeks to months, depending on various factors such as court schedules, whether the tenant contests the eviction, and if there are any delays in the legal proceedings. The court must schedule the First Tier Court Event at least 30 days after the filing of the Summons and Complaint.
   [Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 12: Evictions (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/12%20Evictions%202025.pdf)]

For specific situations or further assistance, it is recommended to consult with an attorney or seek help from legal aid resources such as MassLegalHelp.org or Greater Boston Legal Services.

---
**Sources:**
- [Legal Tactics Ch. 12: Evictions](https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/12%20Evictions%202025.pdf)
- [Legal Tactics Ch. 16: Mobile Homes](https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/16%20Mobile%20Homes%202025.pdf)

results.question: My landlord shut off my hot water to try to get me to move out. What can I do? (MA)
results.response:
In Massachusetts, landlords are required to provide essential services such as hot water. If your landlord has shut off your hot water, they are violating the Massachusetts Sanitary Code, which sets minimum requirements for housing, including the provision of hot water [Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 8: Getting Repairs Made (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/08%20Getting%20Repairs%20Made%202025_0.pdf)].

Here are steps you can take:

1. **Notify Your Landlord**: Inform your landlord in writing about the issue and request that the hot water be restored immediately.

2. **Contact the Board of Health**: If your landlord does not restore the hot water, contact your local Board of Health to request an inspection. A housing inspector can examine your home to see if it follows the state Sanitary Code. If there are violations, an inspector can order your landlord to make repairs within a certain time [Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 8: Getting Repairs Made (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/08%20Getting%20Repairs%20Made%202025_0.pdf)].

3. **Document the Problem**: Take photos or videos of the issue and keep records of all communications with your landlord.

4. **Legal Action**: You may consider taking legal action against your landlord. If you prove the landlord broke the law, a judge may order them to restore services or pay you money for bad conditions [Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 8: Getting Repairs Made (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/08%20Getting%20Repairs%20Made%202025_0.pdf)].

5. **Withhold Rent**: You may have the right to withhold rent until the hot water is restored. However, you should put the withheld rent money in a separate bank account to protect yourself from eviction if your landlord takes you to court. It is advisable to consult with an attorney before withholding rent [Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 8: Getting Repairs Made (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/08%20Getting%20Repairs%20Made%202025_0.pdf)].

For specific legal advice, it is recommended to consult with an attorney. You can also find more resources and assistance at MassLegalHelp.org or contact Greater Boston Legal Services.

results.question: Can my landlord make me pay for professional carpet cleaning when I move out in MA?
results.response:
In Massachusetts, a landlord cannot require a tenant to pay for professional carpet cleaning as a condition of returning a security deposit. According to Massachusetts law, a landlord can only use a security deposit to cover unpaid rent or to repair damages caused by the tenant or their guests beyond normal wear and tear. Routine cleaning, such as carpet cleaning, is generally considered normal wear and tear and should not be deducted from a security deposit.

If you left your apartment in the same condition as you found it, minus reasonable wear and tear, the landlord must return your security deposit balance, with interest, within 30 days after you move out. If the landlord does not return your security deposit, you can send them a demand letter or take legal action in small claims court.

For more detailed information, you can refer to Legal Tactics Chapter 3: Security Deposits [Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 3: Security Deposits (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-10/03%20Security%20Deposits%202025-%20Updated%208-13-25.pdf)].

results.question: I reported code violations and now my landlord is trying to evict me. Is this retaliation? (MA)
results.response:
In Massachusetts, it is illegal for a landlord to evict you in retaliation for reporting code violations. According to Massachusetts law, a landlord cannot evict you, raise your rent, or make a major change in your rental agreement because you reported bad conditions in your apartment or building to the Board of Health or other local officials. This is considered retaliatory eviction, which is prohibited.

To protect yourself, it is important to document your communications with your landlord. Keep copies of any written notices or emails you sent regarding the code violations. If your landlord takes you to court, you will need proof that you reported the violations.

If a court finds that your landlord has retaliated against you, it can order them to pay you up to three months’ rent as damages.

For more detailed guidance, you may want to consult an attorney or reach out to local legal aid resources such as MassLegalHelp.org or Greater Boston Legal Services.

[Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 8: Getting Repairs Made (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/08%20Getting%20Repairs%20Made%202025_0.pdf)]

results.question: What is the Section 8 housing program and how do I apply in Boston?
results.response:
The Section 8 housing program, also known as the Housing Choice Voucher Program, is a federal assistance program that helps low-income families, the elderly, and the disabled afford housing in the private market. Participants receive vouchers that subsidize their rent, allowing them to pay a portion of their income towards rent while the voucher covers the remaining cost.

To apply for Section 8 in Boston, you would typically need to contact the local public housing agency (PHA) that administers the program. In Boston, this is often managed by the Boston Housing Authority (BHA). The application process can vary, but generally involves:

1. **Checking Eligibility**: Eligibility is based on factors such as income, family size, and citizenship status. You must meet the income limits set by the program.

2. **Submitting an Application**: Applications are usually submitted during open enrollment periods. You may need to provide documentation such as proof of income, identification, and information about your household.

3. **Waiting List**: Due to high demand, there is often a waiting list for Section 8 vouchers. The waiting time can vary significantly.

4. **Receiving a Voucher**: Once you receive a voucher, you can search for housing that meets the program's requirements. The housing must pass an inspection to ensure it meets health and safety standards.

For specific guidance and to start the application process, you should contact the Boston Housing Authority or visit their website. Additionally, for more information, you can visit resources like MassLegalHelp.org or Greater Boston Legal Services.

[Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 2: Tenant Screening (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/02%20Tenant%20Screening%202025.pdf)]

results.question: My landlord never gave me a statement of condition for my security deposit. Does this matter? (MA)
results.response:
Yes, it does matter. In Massachusetts, if a landlord takes a security deposit, they are required to provide a Statement of Condition within 10 days of the tenant moving in. This statement should list any existing damage in the apartment. If the landlord fails to provide this, you can create your own Statement of Condition and send it to the landlord, keeping a copy for yourself. This can help protect you if there are disputes about the condition of the apartment when you move out. 

For more information, you can refer to the Legal Tactics guide on security deposits: [Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 3: Security Deposits (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-10/03%20Security%20Deposits%202025-%20Updated%208-13-25.pdf)].

results.question: Can my landlord charge me for bed bug treatment in Massachusetts?
results.response:
In Massachusetts, landlords are generally responsible for maintaining rental units in a habitable condition, which includes addressing pest infestations like bed bugs. According to the Massachusetts Sanitary Code, landlords must ensure that rental properties are free from pests and vermin. Therefore, landlords cannot typically charge tenants for bed bug treatment unless the tenant caused the infestation through their own negligence or actions.

For more detailed information, you can refer to the City of Boston's guidelines on bed bug prevention and extermination, which outline the responsibilities of landlords and tenants in dealing with bed bugs. If you have specific concerns or need assistance, it may be helpful to consult legal aid resources such as MassLegalHelp.org or contact Greater Boston Legal Services.

results.question: Can my landlord keep my security deposit for normal wear and tear in Massachusetts?
results.response:
No, your landlord cannot keep your security deposit for normal wear and tear in Massachusetts. The security deposit can only be used for repairs for damage that are not considered "reasonable wear and tear," unpaid rent, or unpaid real estate tax increase if your contract requires it. Normal wear and tear, such as routine painting, cleaning, and small repairs, cannot be deducted from your security deposit. [Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 3: Security Deposits (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-10/03%20Security%20Deposits%202025-%20Updated%208-13-25.pdf)].

results.question: I'm a domestic violence survivor. Can I break my lease in Massachusetts?
results.response:
Yes, as a survivor of domestic violence in Massachusetts, you may have the right to break your lease early. Massachusetts law provides survivors with the right to break their lease without penalty. This protection is also supported under the federal Violence Against Women Act (VAWA), which prevents survivors of domestic violence from being denied housing or evicted from certain federally funded subsidized housing due to the violence they have experienced. 

For more information, you can refer to the resources provided by MassLegalHelp.org or consult with an attorney for specific guidance on your situation.

results.question: What happens to my lease if my landlord sells the building in Massachusetts?
results.response:
If your landlord sells the building in Massachusetts, the new landlord must honor the agreements in your current lease. This means they must accept your old rent amount until your lease ends, or until they give you proper notice. The new owner cannot raise your rent or change the terms of your lease until the lease period ends or unless they provide proper notice as required by law.

results.question: My landlord is deducting my security deposit for things that were broken when I moved in. What can I do? (MA)
results.response:
In Massachusetts, your landlord is not allowed to deduct from your security deposit for damages that were already present when you moved in. Here are steps you can take:

1. **Document the Condition**: If you have evidence, such as photos or a Statement of Condition, that shows the damages were present when you moved in, gather this documentation. A Statement of Condition should have been provided by your landlord within 10 days of your move-in, or you could have created your own and sent it to your landlord.

2. **Demand Letter**: Send a letter to your landlord requesting the return of your security deposit. You can use a Security Deposit Demand Letter (Form 6) available through resources like MassLegalHelp.org. Include any evidence you have that the damages were pre-existing.

3. **Legal Action**: If your landlord does not return your security deposit, you may consider filing a claim in small claims court. If the landlord is found to have wrongfully withheld your deposit, they may be required to pay you up to three times the amount of the deposit.

For more detailed guidance, you can refer to Chapter 3 of Legal Tactics: Tenants' Rights in Massachusetts, or consult legal aid resources such as MassLegalHelp.org or Greater Boston Legal Services.

[Source: Legal Tactics Ch. 3: Security Deposits (https://www.masslegalhelp.org/sites/default/files/2025-10/03%20Security%20Deposits%202025-%20Updated%208-13-25.pdf)]
